narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 47% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your academic record, including a solid sixth-decile ranking in your first year—slightly better than the typical student's fifth-decile average—but a sharp drop to the first decile in your third year, well below the average fifth-decile mark. Your LSAT score of 26.5 is notably lower than the dataset average of 36.7, your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 falls short of the typical 3.21, and your middle family income aligns roughly with averages, while being male and white matches the most common profiles among past students. You also plan to attend part-time, which is less common than the full-time path taken by over 92% of students in our data. The model's decision was driven most strongly by your third-year decile ranking, as your low performance there significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Next in influence was your first-year decile, which, despite being decent, still contributed toward the failure outlook in the model's eyes. Your LSAT score ranked third in impact, with its below-average level further tilting things negatively, followed closely by family income, racial background, undergraduate GPA, and part-time status, all nudging the prediction toward failure to varying degrees. Interestingly, your gender as male provided a very slight counterbalance, pushing mildly in favor of passing compared to others. Overall, these factors combined to make your profile riskier than typical students who pass the bar, especially given how your later academic slide stands out against stable performers. To improve your future chances, focus on boosting your academic deciles through stronger study habits or tutoring, as grades in later years were the top influencers here. Consider retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above the 36.7 average, since it played a key role. Switching to full-time study could also help align you with successful patterns, and building a higher GPA trajectory will make the biggest difference for bar success down the line. With targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor—keep pushing forward.
